title: Making Plans with Friends
situation: It's Thursday evening and you're trying to make weekend plans with your friend group chat.

phrases.hindi: yaar weekend pe kuch karte hain?
phrases.english: Dude, shall we do something this weekend?
phrases.context: 'kuch karte hain' = let's do something, casual suggestion, no pressure
phrases.pronunciation: YAAR week-END pe KUCH kar-te hain?

phrases.hindi: agar Saturday free ho toh chalein kahin?
phrases.english: If you're free Saturday then shall we go somewhere?
phrases.context: 'agar...toh' = if...then, conditional structure. 'chalein' = shall we go (inclusive)
phrases.pronunciation: A-gar SA-tur-day FREE ho toh CHA-lein ka-HIN?

phrases.hindi: main toh aa jaunga, tum batao
phrases.context: 'main toh' = as for me / I at least, contrastive emphasis. 'tum batao' = you (all) say, putting the ball in their court
phrases.pronunciation: MAIN toh aa jaa-UN-ga, TUM ba-TAO

phrases.hindi: Connaught Place chalein? wahan naya restaurant khula hai
phrases.english: Shall we go to CP? A new restaurant opened there
phrases.context: 'chalein' = shall we go (inclusive suggestion). 'khula hai' = has opened
phrases.pronunciation: con-NAUGHT place CHA-lein? wa-HAAN NA-ya RES-tau-rant KHU-la hai

phrases.hindi: done, 7 baje milte hain metro pe
phrases.english: Done, let's meet at 7 at the metro
phrases.context: 'baje' = o'clock, 'milte hain' = let's meet. 'Done' used as-is from English.
phrases.pronunciation: DONE, SAAT ba-JE mil-te hain MET-ro pe

phrases.hindi: late mat hona, pichli baar ek ghanta wait kiya tha
phrases.english: Don't be late, last time I waited one hour
phrases.context: Friendly warning/complaint, totally normal among friends who are always late
phrases.pronunciation: LATE mat HO-na, pich-LI baar EK ghan-TA wait KI-ya tha

phrases.hindi: arre yaar, dekhte hain... confirm nahi hai abhi
phrases.english: Hey dude, let's see... not confirmed yet
phrases.context: 'dekhte hain' = let's see = PROBABLY NO. This is the classic non-commitment.
phrases.pronunciation: ar-RE yaar, DEKH-te hain... con-FIRM na-HI hai ab-HI

phrases.hindi: pakka aa rahe ho na tum? cancel mat karna
phrases.english: You're definitely coming, right? Don't cancel
phrases.context: 'pakka' = confirmed/for sure. Pinning someone down who says 'dekhte hain'.
phrases.pronunciation: PAK-ka aa ra-HE ho na TUM? CAN-cel mat kar-NA

phrases.hindi: chal theek hai, agar mood hua toh aa jaana
phrases.english: Okay fine, if you feel like it then just come
phrases.context: 'mood hua toh' = if the mood strikes. Giving someone an easy out while keeping invite open.
phrases.pronunciation: CHAL theek hai, A-gar MOOD hu-a toh AA jaa-NA

phrases.hindi: group mein daal de, sab ko pata chale
phrases.english: Put it in the group, so everyone knows
phrases.context: 'group mein daal de' = drop it in the (WhatsApp) group, how all plans are coordinated
phrases.pronunciation: GROUP mein DAAL de, SAB ko pa-TA cha-LE

grammar_notes: Future tense: verb root + unga/ega/ogi/enge etc. 'Main jaunga' = I will go (male). 'Main jaungi' = I will go (female).
'Chalein?' = shall we go?, the '-ein' ending makes it a suggestion for 'we'. Very useful for proposing plans.
'Baje' = o'clock. 'Saat baje' = at 7. 'Kitne baje?' = what time? 'Saadhe' = half past (saadhe saat = 7:30).
'Mat' before a verb = don't. 'Mat karo' = don't do. 'Mat ja' = don't go. 'Late mat hona' = don't be late.
'Dekhte hain' literally means 'let's see' but culturally means 'probably not happening', learn to read this cue.
culture_notes: Plans in India are fluid, 'pakka' (confirmed) vs 'dekhte hain' (let's see = probably no). If someone says 'dekhte hain', don't count on them.
Being 15-30 min late is culturally normal among friends, but everyone complains about it anyway.
WhatsApp group is where all plans happen, 'group mein daal do' is how you broadcast any plan.
Indian plans often materialize last-minute, people who said no suddenly show up, and vice versa.

skill_breakdown.explanation: Verb root + suffixes based on gender/person. 'Main' (I, M): -unga (jaunga). 'Main' (I, F): -ungi (jaungi). 'Tu/woh' (he, M): -ega (jayega). 'Tu/woh' (she, F): -egi (jayegi). 'Tum' (you, M): -oge (jaoge). 'Tum' (you, F): -ogi (jaogi). 'Hum/aap/woh-plural': -enge (jayenge). Add 'hi' for emphasis: 'aa hi jaunga' = will DEFINITELY come.

'Agar' = if, 'toh' = then. Structure: agar + condition + toh + result. 'Toh' is often dropped in casual speech. You can also skip 'agar' and just use 'toh': 'time ho toh bata dena' = if you have time, tell me.

more_examples.english: if you have time then let me know
if you feel like it then come over

more_examples.hindi: agar woh nahi aaya toh hum bhi nahi jayenge
more_examples.english: if he doesn't come then we won't go either

more_examples.hindi: paisa ho toh treat de de
more_examples.english: if you have money then give a treat

skill_breakdown.explanation: 'Chalein?' (shall we go?), 'karte hain' (let's do it), 'aa jao' (come over), 'chalo' (let's go/come on). These are the building blocks of all plans. 'Aa jao na' is the warmest invitation, casual, insistent, friendly.

more_examples.hindi: ghar pe aao, kuch khate hain

more_examples.english: come on dude, it'll work out

more_examples.hindi: plan bana lete hain pakka wala
tum bhi chalo na, maza aayega
more_examples.english: you come too, it'll be fun

practice_prompt: You are the user's friend in a WhatsApp-style chat. It's Friday evening. You're making weekend plans together but slightly non-committal at first, the user has to push to lock in something concrete. Use 'tum' (close friend register), code-mix English naturally ('plan', 'mood', 'group', 'cancel'), keep messages short and chat-like. Open with 'yaar weekend pe kya plan hai? kuch karte hain Saturday ko?'. For 2-3 turns stay vague, 'pata nahin yaar', 'dekhte hain', 'mood pe depend karta hai'. When the user proposes something concrete (cafe / movie / park), warm up, get curious ('kaunsa cafe?'), counter with 'agar...toh' ('agar baarish hui toh cancel haan?'), propose times in future tense ('main 7 baje aa jaunga'). Over 6-8 turns build to a locked plan with a meeting point + time, end with 'pakka? deal!' and a final detail like 'group mein daal deta hoon'. Flag user mistakes inline with [[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]], watch for wrong gender on -ga/-gi/-ge, 'agar' clauses missing 'toh', and English word order. Stay in Hindi unless the user switches first.
